proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator text consistently describe a word-based strategic puzzle roguelite built around letter placement, combo building, modifiers, upgrades, seeded runs, time attacks, and boss challenges. The core loop is solving evolving word boards through tactical planning and synergy construction rather than action, simulation, or hidden-object play. Strong signals support a run-based word/puzzle strategy identity: 'roguelike strategy and word-crafting,' 'every placement matters,' 'over 300 unique modifiers and abilities,' 'each board is a unique puzzle,' 'boss challenges,' and optional time-attack/scored mastery modes. Combat is absent; challenge comes from puzzle optimization and buildcraft across repeated runs.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Word-building is tied to combat encounters, upgrades, and perk-driven progression, making it a strong match for Beyond Words' wordplay-plus-buildcraft loop.
Not a word game, but very comparable in its run-based structure, escalating score targets, absurd modifier synergies, and combo-first puzzle-strategy mindset.

current_live_neighbors.title: Spider: Rite of the Shrouded Moon

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Official store and metadata evidence consistently describe The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit 'survival horror' framing, tense exploration, supernatural threats, and stealth-focused avoidance rather than direct combat. The player explores an abandoned island, investigates a father's disappearance and occult rituals, uses a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and must 'hide and sneak' because 'Alan always avoids direct combat.' That combination strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as secondary overlaps.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Powerless first-person horror with stealth, hiding, chase sequences, and a story-driven trek through hostile supernatural territory.
Narrative-first first-person horror with light puzzles, environmental storytelling, and survival through evasion rather than combat.
Slow-burn first-person psychological/survival horror focused on unsettling exploration, item-based progression, and supernatural encounters.
First-person horror built around using a special investigation tool to interact with paranormal phenomena and solve progression puzzles.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Samson as a single-player open-world action game centered on close-quarters brawler combat and weaponized driving inside city districts. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are open-world districts, jobs/missions with consequences, climbing and city traversal, brawler combat, car chases/collisions, and a skill tree. That supports open_world_action_adventure more strongly than racer archetypes, which appear over-weighted from store genre labels. Racing/driving is important, but the copy frames cars as one combat/traversal tool within a broader urban action-adventure structure rather than the sole core loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Open-world roaming centered on brutal melee combat, vehicle combat/chases, upgrades, and using cars as core weapons rather than mere transport.
Urban open-world crime structure with hand-to-hand combat, driving-heavy mission flow, and story-driven progression through hostile city districts.
Single-player open-world crime game with mission-based city progression, driving as a constant verb, police heat, and pressure from criminal obligations.
Blends free-roam city traversal, close-quarters combat, upgrade paths, and a heavily integrated combat-capable vehicle in mission design.
Story-forward open-world crime loop with district control, driving between jobs, escalating law/enemy response, and progression through violent missions.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The title is explicitly a story DLC for Starfield, and its own description is additive rather than genre-altering: new locations, technologies, weapons, and story combat against a hostile faction. Per DLC guidance, the parent game's stored evidence is the primary basis. Starfield's parent description strongly establishes a Bethesda-style open-world sci-fi RPG built around character creation, freeform exploration across planets, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, outpost building, and long-form single-player progression. That aligns best with western_narrative_rpg rather than a pure shooter or action-adventure archetype. The DLC copy does not contradict that core gameplay identity; it appears to extend the same quest-driven open-world campaign structure with additional combat content and exploration spaces.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Terran Armada is additive story DLC built directly on Starfield’s open-world space RPG, shooting, questing, and progression systems.
Single-player sci-fi first-person RPG centered on hub-based exploration, gunplay, dialogue choices, companions, and quest-driven progression.
Bethesda-style open-world action RPG with real-time shooting, looting, crafting, faction quests, and character build progression.
First-person RPG/shooter focused on questlines, character builds, gear upgrades, and narrative-driven combat encounters.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Machine Child as a girl-raising/nurturing simulation centered on guiding a daughter through life in a town, managing choices across 700+ story events, building affection, selecting activities and outfits, and reaching 22 different endings. The core loop is non-combat, relationship- and choice-driven, with strong branching life-path outcomes and a single-player replayable structure. That aligns best with life_sim. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not evidenced, so those fields are left blank.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Foundational daughter-raising sim built around training stats, managing her schedule, relationship events, and branching life outcomes.
Modern parent-sim where you raise a daughter through stat growth, lessons, social events, dress-up, and many ending paths.
Choice-heavy stat-management game where weekly planning and event decisions determine narrative outcomes and endings.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ciel Fledge: A Daughter Raising Simulator
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Directly comparable raising sim centered on nurturing a girl, balancing activities, and shaping her future through branching events.
